result:
If there's one thing that captivates me, it’s the quiet persistence of roots. 

Think about a tree: we admire its height, the spread of its branches, the rustle of its leaves. But beneath the soil, hidden from sight, there’s another world entirely—a tangled, patient architecture. Roots don’t hurry. They feel their way through darkness, navigating stones and clay, seeking nourishment in places no light ever touches. They hold the soil together; they whisper to fungi in a symbiotic tongue we’re only beginning to understand.

It makes me wonder about our own lives. So much of what we value is visible—achievements, appearance, the personas we craft for the world. But what about our hidden roots? The private struggles, the quiet disciplines, the unseen acts of kindness, the inner conversations that shape who we are? They go unnoticed, often by design. Yet they determine whether we stand firm in the storm or wither when the wind picks up.

We live in an age that celebrates the blossom and ignores the root. We want quick growth, instant results, constant flowering. But a tree that flowers nonstop will exhaust itself. There are seasons for growing inward, downward, into the depths. There is wisdom in the unseen.

Maybe that’s why I find such solace in writing—or in any act of genuine creation. It’s a way of tending to those roots. You sit with your thoughts, your memories, your questions, and you allow them to intertwine beneath the surface of daily life. What emerges above ground—the poem, the story, the insight—is just the visible part. The real work happened in the dark.

And perhaps the most beautiful thing about roots is that they connect. In a forest, trees communicate through vast underground networks. They share resources; they send warnings. They are not solitary. It’s a gentle reminder that even our most hidden selves are part of something larger. Our private growth, our silent endurance, eventually touches others in ways we may never fully see.

So here’s to the roots—in the earth, in the soul, in the heart of things. May we have the courage to grow downward when needed, to trust the darkness, and to remember that what holds us up is often what goes unseen.
